transcript.text: How's it going?
Hey Bianca, how are you?
I'm doing great.
Happy Friday.
Yes, I'm about to say the exact same thing.
Whereabouts are you located?
Seattle currently.
Oh, you're in Seattle?
Okay, cool.
West Coast.
I'm glad I didn't grab you at the end of your day if on the East Coast.
I'm in Oakland.
We have an office in San Francisco.
So we have a couple people on the team here.
I'm super excited to chat with you.
So, know, typically I just start giving a little bit of context.
I think you talked with Tucker already a little bit.
So I'll give you some information there.
But I think the coldest call really, I just want to share kind of what we're doing, what we're looking for, and just see if it's something you're interested in working on.
I'm sure you have a bunch of questions to make that judgment for yourself.
Would that work for you?
Yeah, absolutely.
Works perfectly fine.
Okay, awesome.
So, I mean, we're GrowthX.
We are essentially an agency where our customers kind of outsource people.
So their growth function to us.
So, you know, for example, one of my clients, Webflow, they outsource their SEO and content marketing function where the goal is to like build awareness with developers.
And then our whole angle is like we kind of pair humans with AI workflows and a crazy engineering team that's able to build them to deliver faster, you know, higher throughput, more.
And because of that, you know, we give them almost like more strategy with like really senior people that you just really wouldn't get at all working with any other agency.

And then the ultimate goal of the company is to kind of use this agency that we're building called GrowthX as kind of a proving ground to figure out like, hey, you know, engineers have been working in this amazing ecosystem of like tools where they've just because of AI, like, you know, rocketed in terms of productivity.
But like everyone else, I think, is still stuck in like Google Sheets and like Google Docs.
You know, and all files everywhere, taking the same work over and over again.
There has to be a better way.
And we're trying to build a product that's able to do that.
And then here, I lead GoToMarket, which is half working with clients.
So some of my clients are like Webflow, Abnormal Security, some kind of like cloud GPU companies.

And then the other half, I'm responsible for growing GrowthX, the company, and also growing the umbrella company, which we haven't even named yet, that, you know, will release this product and spin up all these other new services as well.
So I spent a lot of time on marketing.
I spent a lot of time also spinning up new kind of like services, or I guess for us, it's like half service, half product, zero to one.
Like right now, we do a lot of SEO, but we also want to do other things, you know, like outbound or social media or, you know, link building, which is kind of under SEO.
Yeah, so that's kind of what we do.
And then our need is basically on, honestly, on everything, but like on the growing growthx side, there's just so much to do there.
That's all kind of zero to one.
And we're just looking for somebody that can help us or help me and work with me to kind of make that happen.

And I could share some of the projects, but then a little bit about me, my backgrounds, basically doing this in some form for the last decade from small startups like Kite, which is like Cursor and some of these like code, you know, AIs version zero back in 2019 to some larger companies like Affirm.
And then I also had my own YC back startup, which is in the DevTool space, which I think was one of the things that really interested me about kind of your background.
have a like really rare mix of like marketer, but then also probably technical a little bit and entrepreneurial.
Yeah, and then I met Marcel.

Well, because I was a power user for this app called AirOps that, you know, probably one of the better ones for, like, doing this sort of work, like AI plus humans for kind of marketing growth operations.
And when I heard what he was doing, I was like, all right, I'm already convinced, you know, I'm already bought in.
Like, let me help you grow this thing.
Yeah, so that's kind of growthx.me, what we're looking for.
I would love to hear more about you if you're willing to share and, like, kind of where you're at.
Was there anything about this that was interesting?
Do you have any questions?
You know, maybe we can start there.
Yeah, sure.
Happy to start there.
Makes a ton of sense.
Yeah, definitely take a look at the website.
Pretty cool stuff.
Looks like you're moving rapidly.
So that's also very cool to see.
I tend to speak quickly.
So if I speak too fast, just let me know and I'll go back to it.
But yes, very non-traditional marketers.
So you're spot on there.
I found my way there in an interesting path.
So I was a Make-A-Wish kid, so I was very, very sick growing up.
I wished for a MacBook when I was 17 years old and a DLSR camera.
You learned photography and said, hey, I'm going to learn some skills so that if I ever get out of this, I can go and have a job.
So that's my first path into marketing.
I ended up getting into college, got an internship with the United States Olympic Committee, and that's where I kind of first touched marketing.
So for context, I wanted to be a pediatric nurse.
I was like, hey, that's where I should do.
I live it.
I know the universe, but that's not – I had a college advisor, so I didn't have to do that just because I was a sick kid.
So let's try marketing.
So studied sports management, had too many credits to actually go and study marketing or business.
So then picked up a lot of the sporting committees, got multiple sclerosis in the middle of my Olympic internship.
So it was super bummer.
Came from this childhood past of tons and tons of hospitals, and so now having it again in my old life, lost mobility during that internship.
I was in a wheelchair.
They were trying to –
Do adaptive showers and things like that.
so I was like, hey, I'm going to Silicon Valley.
I'm going to book a one-way ticket.
I'm going to San Francisco.
Let's go.
I've got one shot.
Let's go do it.
So I to San Francisco.
Originally, my first role was in Mountain View.
I was a contractor to Accenture at Facebook.

So I was leading a team of 10 contractors, doing a little bit of like, I would say not modeling, but more so like a little bit of client review plus projections on the number of duplicate profiles on the platform.
Loved it.
I didn't know anything about startups.
didn't know anything about venture capital or the language, but I was exposed to it.
So I rode Caltrain.
I heard people talk.
I saw all the backpacks.
And that's when I first started to see, okay, cool.
Like, this is actually exciting.
I like it.
I may want to work in this.
So I loved Facebook.
It was awesome.
Got to see the big tech, all the cool food and things like that.
Had a great time, but then realized that I wanted to be a builder.
And I wanted to kind of build something like that myself.
I wanted to be in the early days and kind of like, you know, help build a foundation for something new.
So they are caught a little
I joined Bot Society as the first North American employee, also first employee outside of the Founders.
They were based in Milan.
I opened up our San Francisco office, I would say 2017.
It over in Golden Gate, not the best area.
My Golden Gate Theater, not the best area, but that was great.
It was pre-revenue.
It was like $200,000 that we'd raised, so not very much funding.
Yeah, hit the ground running.
So I was hired there as a junior marketer, a very junior role.
Yeah, we did a lot of stuff.
Grew a community to 3,000 people of UX, UI designers in San Francisco.
We partnered with Mozilla, Facebook, Google, you name it.
Had tons of intimate meetings and meetups.
Really, really enjoyed the work.
So eventually took over all of marketing, hired a team, managed about two people at once.
We had some contractors as well.
So I would say like somewhere around four, but two full-time.
Yeah, things went great.
Built up some momentum, ended up getting an investment from the Google Assistant Investment Fund.
Which ultimately acquired Bot Society.
And so then I went on to my next journey, which was at MIG School.
That's where I began to touch on some of the development computer science stuff, which I grew to love.
I was hired as a digital marketing manager right after their series B.
So they kind of brought me in to kind of take that momentum off that last race, grow student enrollment, student growth using digital channels, paid acquisition, paid social.
We did great.
So yeah, it was a larger team.
My first time working in it, like I said, a larger, more mature team.
With more robust funding.
But eventually we were using a non-traditional funding model called income share agreements to fund the students.
And so that kind of wiped us out.
The students ended up suing the school.
School had to shut down.
so kind of that was the end of MIG School.
From there, I went to CodePath.
I don't know if CodePath is on my resume, but I was the first digital marketer at CodePath or YC back nonprofit aiming to get people expertise to that computer science.
Like if you're a computer science student in the first year, you don't touch a computer.
And so if you're from a low income background or, you know, first generation.
It can be kind of like, you get let down.
You you think you're going to be working with computers and building apps, but if you don't do that, then it's like, okay, what am I studying?
So we get them project-based curriculum early.
So Lyft, Spotify, all these cool companies would partner with us for summer programs.
The students would get to touch computers, they'd like it, and they'd stick around.
So yeah, it was cool.
So I was recruited by Aris to become VP of growth during my time at CodePath.
So I didn't stay long, but I ended up bringing in someone in my network to take my role or replace me.
And that's when I joined Aris.
Again, no revenue, $0 in MRR, AR, nothing.
Was the first person outside of the founders.
They were still at Babson.
So the entrepreneur school, all of them came from Babson.
So very much an entrepreneurial mindset.
Yeah.
So we had to start, I built out, you know, a pilot program that ultimately converted into a contract, an annual contract.
So that's how we started.
We were very much a services company at that time.
So we were taking all of these like marketing materials or not marketing materials, but like learning.
Materials and changing them into different formats, such as text message, SMS, WhatsApp, Teams, Slack, et cetera.
But we were just doing the work ourselves.
Like we were the content people.
So we were taking all these hundreds of documents and doing it, but that wasn't recurring revenue.
So we had to kill that, kill services, actually get a SaaS model, helped hire the first AEs, managed the first AEs and SDRs.
Yeah, got all the way to Series A.
So we had almost a two-year run, was able to get like 6 million in pipeline, 2.3 in closed sales.
And then I got sick for a little bit.
So I had to take some time off.
Once the team, we raised our Series A, I was there, you know, we had, we were opening up a little satellite office or a hub.
I felt like the team was in a good place and because of the health stuff, I could take some time away.
So that's when I left Airst and kind of took a little break before joining the next role, which was Humanly.
Humanly was awesome.
That's what brought me to Seattle.
We were a seed stage company before 1 million in revenue, so very small, but they hired with the expectation.
that we were going to raise a bridge.
The bridge was not scary.
I can work with small budgets.
I'm not someone that has to have a massive budget.
I'm not going to go on a spending spree, so that didn't scare me.
We hit the ground running.
Things went really well.
Raised a very strong Series A.
Loved it.
Opened up a physical office.
When I joined, we were fully remote, so we opened up a physical office.
I helped open the office and then kind of just didn't like the culture and what was happening internally.

I felt like a lot of it was very sales and marketing focused.
A lot of the product wasn't there.
I was marketing something.
I didn't know if it was actually real.
I just didn't like the way that it felt.
After we raised our Series A similar scenario, I felt like, hey, they're in a good place.
They need someone more senior, I think, right now.
I'm in a place in my career where I'd like to learn and work with someone.
Being a one-person marketing army is fun, but it also eventually gets tired.
Being able to have a larger team to bounce ideas off of.
And brainstorm was very exciting to me.
So left humanly, no bad blood, talked to Prem yesterday, just raised a momentum round.
So not a series B, but an in-between.
And then Gitstart happened.
So Gitstart, I did not actually apply for Gitstart.
I had my own startup.
I had my own ideas and projects and things like that.
So I pitched a bunch of YC founders and I said, hey, I'm offering fractional marketing services.
I'm building my own thing.
And so Gitstart just completely ignored my pitch.
And so they just put me in the hiring funnel.
And when I ended up interviewing with them, they were really smart people.
And I respected that.
So loved it, loved the team, ended up taking them on.
It was definitely a pay cut.
So I went backwards a little bit, but because they were super smart, I didn't really mind it.
Ultimately, the low-code, no-code solutions, the replets, the lovables, the bolts kind of just killed a lot.
Vibe coding kind of just wiped it out.
Like we were kind of a services company.
We weren't really SaaS.
And so once all of those tools came out, it just, yeah, a lot of churn.
And currently Gitstart is now on one of my...
Clients.
I'm no longer there full-time, but I still have a great relationship, learned a ton, lots of access to developers, and yeah, happy to answer your questions along the way.
Yeah, I mean, wait, so you're saying Gitstart was kind of hurt by all the kind of lovables and coding tools?
Exactly, because our biggest marketing, I guess our tagline was like, you only pay for what you merge.
So if we give you a PR and you don't merge it, you don't pay for it.
But like, that was a big magic moment.
And so now people can do that themselves and create workflows to do that without really needing Gitstart.
Ironically, GoodCall, which is one, I think, one of the growthx customers, he's one of the testimonials on the website.
So I did a testimonial with him at Gitstart, and he was one of our big clients.
And again, same type of thing where it's just like, okay, now that I have all this AI functionality, like I said, we were really big on human in the loop.
So like, yes, you've got your engineering team, and yes, you have your architects, and they know what they're doing.
But like, this is about 10x in your velocity.
And so yeah, it just, yeah.
Got it.
That's, yeah, that's interesting.
impacted that part.
I almost wouldn't have, it seems like such a secondary effect of like, I guess when I use Lovable, I don't need to submit PRs.

Yeah, and I think a lot of it was like the flashiness of AI tools and like kind of taking a pause of just like, let's stop everything that's like kind of innovative that we don't, we're not sure of.
Let's take a step back, let's wait a little bit, and then make decisions.
And so it was just like timing and yeah.
Fair enough.
And I guess like for you, like what sorts of things do you enjoy working on?
I guess now that you've been in all these places.

love the ability to have cool ideas and experiment with them, but also being very data driven.

I like to build, I built a ton of projects.
I would say like 50 projects or more.
Over the last six months.
So creating content, SEO, building SEO for agents.

Paid search can do that.
What do I like?
Brand, love brand.
I know that's not necessarily huge for a growth marketer.

Like, that's the way I like to think.
Yeah, I mean, that's, that's awesome.
I think like, brand to me, I think I didn't feel like this, because same with you, you know, I started in the world of like, very quantitative, measurable, kind of growth hacking, almost.

It's tough, because you can't really measure it super well.
But like, you know, at least here, I think we have like full faith in that.

Sure.

I guess you mentioned the kind of bring AI first principles.

Yeah, I like the idea of it being kind of like an agency and a SaaS solution together.
I do have a background in agency.

So yeah, very flexible.
I've also had my own agency.

So I enjoyed doing that.
What would I like to do?
Yeah, I would like to get back into that.
I like the fact that it does have that agency component, so there is a level of sell here.
And so creating assets that can get the sales team to just really, really close these deals easily, creating assets that CS can use to upsell.
I think that's where I can really get things going and move quickly.
And also being able to identify their pain points and getting very targeted, to your point.
In the early days, you do things that don't scale.
And I know like when you think about growth, that's like scary.
But if you do it the right way, then you're going to scale rapidly.
So really focusing on creating that cornerstone content, the sales enablement materials.
I think that would be huge.
But yeah, I can do everything.
I think learning a little bit more about what your clients look like.
I know I saw a few of them on the website, but how are they finding you?
Is it mostly like word of mouth?
And if it is word of mouth, then I can jump in and do a referral push and we build out a referral program.
Like, yeah, so a little bit of everything.
know that's kind of an all over the place answer, but yeah.
Well, I mean, I think that's good because working here is a little bit of everything.
I guess from my side, I was thinking through like, you know, depending on the person, they could focus on any of these three areas.

Like for me, like I categorize them, like, it's like the client side of like, you know, essentially we're the partner of like usually some director or VP level person at these companies to drive some outcome around growth.

area like pods.

think spending up services zero to one is another area.
Like right now, for example, the two that I'm spending the most time on is kind of like thought leadership, social media ghostwriting almost as a service.

So like, you if you're a founder of a small company, I think increasingly now, like building a brand and getting kind of leads that are really qualified and have the right kind of idea in their mind is like huge, like from LinkedIn and Twitter.
But most of them just like don't have time or they don't have that muscle of like just posting and putting yourself out there.
So we have that as a service and then link building.
I mean, sounds like you've spent a little bit of time there, but like link building is just this really dumb place where like you buy links from these brokers.
And for me, I think like, especially with AI now, link building should be something more like I want to wrap it in this like almost distribution.

Sort of framing, like, hey, you've written the content, what are you actually doing to distribute it, you know, and if you, you know, paid much attention to, like, all the ISEO, you know, LLM citation stuff, like, it's, like, increasingly important now that the person, like, you know, you as the company, it's not the only person that's, like, shouting, like, hey, we're really good as a tool, you know, even if you rank first on Google.
So, you know, that's another area, like, spinning up workstreams zero to one.
And then the fuzziest, like, weirdest area is just growing this company.
Right now, it's growing growthx a little bit, but, like, you know, after this call here right now, if you go to ai-ledgrowth.com, it's, like, a community we're spinning up.
Like, we're just very, kind of, broad in thinking about what it looks like to grow this company.
And there's just a ton of projects there, from launching community to micro tools to eventually exposing, like, pieces of this product we're building.
yeah.
And how to kind of take that to market.

About the AI-led growth, is this the product that's going to be announced soon?
No, it's not really a product.
So like how I guess we're thinking about that is like growthx, the agency, it's kind of expensive, right?
It's like starts at 9,000.
I think on average, our customers pay us about like 14k a month.

But there's just like an insane number of people and companies that can't pay that, that want to learn and adopt these kind of just like strategies, you know, to get more out of like every dollar and every human hour you're spending in marketing growth.
So this was kind of one of the ways we wanted to kind of solve that problem and also just capitalize on that.
You know, because a lot of people that come to us.
Can't pay 9k, but they will pay like $500, you know, for a course and be a part of a community.
And eventually when they've grown, they'll come and, you know, get our growthx service and we can even hire from that pool.
So this is kind of like, almost, I guess, a segment of our product, you could think.
But it's very much a community, like I would compare it to like, for sales, there's a community called like Exit 5.
There's a program called Go-To-Market Engineering School.
I think it's like our way of evangelizing the move to using AI for growth.
It's like a way to kind of like segment our audience and give them something that doesn't cost 15k a month.
And it's also, I don't know, just like, I feel like you should probably think it through a little bit more, but in general, that's why we're doing it.
It's almost like an experiment, you know, to see if it'll, it'll drive some meaningful kind of revenue or growth for us.
Yeah.
Do you have any level of referral yet of just like people that are finding you or does that just?
So I think we definitely do have referrals.
I would say it's not very like systematic.
I would say like mostly how we've gotten our sales so far is, or at least the initial ones is Marcel at the very beginning ran a bunch of workshops on using AI for SEO.
And that turned into that initial batch of customers.
And we've continued to kind of run these workshops and like provide value.
And that initial group of customers and these workshops really is like the main way we've been sourcing all of our customers.
Marcel's background is just like really deep into marketing, you know, CMO at scale and marketing leader at like HashiCorp.
So you kind of just has like an insane network that we've been, you know, closing customers from.
Like to me, I think the brand is much more important.
Because growthx, the agency, it probably can have 200 or 300 customers, but I don't think growthx, the agency, is ever going to have 10,000 customers because we're treating it as a proving ground.
So it's much more important to me that the story of growthx is solid and distributed versus we have this crazy funnel of just leads, leads, leads everywhere.
We almost can't handle it.
We've been slowing down our new client onboarding because we don't have the resources to take on more.
Yeah.
No, that's spot on.
Storytelling is huge.
I think that's something I personally really enjoy a lot.
Using different video formats.
I know everyone has a podcast these days, but I've done different video series.
At Bot Society, we did Bot Society for Education, and so we partnered with the computer science lab at Brown.

And so I saw them in an intercom.

And getting people to be human and say that this stuff is real.
I like to humanize products.
I think it's paramount right now.
I think that is very much the flavor we're going for.

Justin, Justin Walsh dot me.
I don't know if you've ever seen this guy.

It's like a service or a friend, you know, I think I'm much more inspired by that.

It's not like, like, there's no product there, you know, it's just like, be within this group of people.
Cool.
Yeah, I mean, I know I only have two minutes, I'll breeze through the rest, please send me questions if you have any.

But generally, I guess how I was thinking about, you know, doing this process is like, we'll just have literally one more step, and it'll be kind of a mini like, project shouldn't take more than like, two hours, I would say.

It'll probably be like some zero to one project.
The scope of all these things I've mentioned.
then, yeah, and then after that, like, I mean, we'll give some sort of offer.

I guess, again, sorry about the timing, but like, I wanted to ask you like, what you wanted is full time, even what you want, you know, because you're at marketer hire right now and like, what your expectations are there.
But yeah, maybe we can schedule another 10 minute call or, you know, we have one more minute.
Is there any questions you have, like, that can help you just think through is it interesting and something you want to explore further?
Yeah.
What's been the most surprising thing since you've joined?
I think, I mean, I kind of thought about it before I joined, but like, I think the form products take when they go to market is like important, right?
Like, I think as far as this AI boom is concerned, we've seen a lot of hype marketing around products that like tell you they're magical, but it's.
I feel like at the moment, that is the wrong go-to-market.
Like having a self-serve product that people can use almost forces you to kind of market in that way.
And reality is almost never that.
Whereas I think the go-to-market of growthx honestly could be the future of like no more products, no more like SaaS.
Like you're buying an outcome and you're buying like a service and how it's delivered.
Who cares, right?
Like in the background, it could be all me or it could be none of me and all AI.
Like that shouldn't matter to you as a customer.
And I think it was surprising.
I don't know if intentional that like because of this go-to-market, like it's just so much easier to grow and build.
Like for us, we could rip and replace a tool out that we built ourselves or that we're using and the customers wouldn't even feel it.
